fed_monetary: The FOMC voted 8-4 on April 29 to hold rates at 3.50–3.75% for the third consecutive meeting — the largest dissent count since 1992 — with three members preferring a cut and one opposing the easing bias in the statement, reflecting acute internal divergence on the policy path. Chair Powell, whose term expires May 15, confirmed he will remain on the Board as a governor, while Kevin Warsh's nomination cleared the Senate Banking Committee 13-11 and a full Senate floor vote is expected the week of May 11, introducing immediate regime-change risk to forward guidance. With core PCE running at 3.2% against a 2.8% estimate and PPI at 6.0% — more than double the 2.6% consensus — the case for near-term easing has all but collapsed, and CME FedWatch traders have begun pricing a modest probability of a hike in the back half of 2026.
labor_data: The March 2026 employment situation remains the most recent read, with NFP printing +178K — a strong beat versus the +100K estimate — and the unemployment rate holding at 4.3%, in line with consensus. Initial jobless claims for the latest week came in at 189K, well below the 210K estimate, suggesting the labor market continues to resist tariff-driven demand softness and has not yet transmitted goods-sector stress into broad-based layoffs. The April employment situation report is due May 8 and will be the critical test of whether tariff headwinds — estimated at a 5-point drag on corporate EPS — are beginning to erode hiring intentions.
fiscal_supply: Treasury's Q2 2026 quarterly refunding announcement is scheduled for May 4, with financing estimates and TBAC documents expected to reset the market's supply calendar; auction sizes in Q1 held steady at approximately $125B in total refunding, and any upward revision would pressure the 10Y yield, currently at 4.42%, toward the top of consensus fair-value ranges. The TGA stands at $981.9B, providing a comfortable near-term buffer, while the RRP at $8.3B is essentially drained, meaning incremental Treasury issuance will be absorbed almost entirely by bank reserves — currently $2,918.6B — without an offsetting RRP release to cushion duration impact. Tariff-driven fiscal dynamics remain ambiguous: while higher import levies boost customs receipts near-term, structural deficit imbalances require sustained long-end supply, keeping term premium sticky.
corporate: HY OAS at 283 bps and IG OAS at 102 bps remain historically tight, implying markets are not yet pricing a credit deterioration scenario consistent with PPI running 340 bps above consensus or the tariff regime's documented 5-point headwind to core EPS growth. Q1 2026 GDP printed at 2.0% annualized — below the 2.3% consensus and sharply above the 0.5% pace in Q4 2025 — driven by a rebound in government spending, but the composition reflects tariff front-running in goods imports that may reverse in Q2. Risk appetite is contained but not fearful: the VIX at 16.63 and SPX at 7,255 (+0.63%) suggest equities are pricing a soft-landing outcome, though the NFCI at -0.518 and ANFCI at -0.49 indicate accommodative financial conditions — a potential source of complacency given the stagflationary inflation-growth mix.
next_catalyst: The single most important near-term event is the May 4 Treasury Quarterly Refunding Announcement, which will set auction sizes for Q2 2026 and could reprice the long end if borrowing estimates are revised higher; this coincides with the May 8 April Employment Situation release, making the first full week of May a pivotal double-catalyst window for rates and risk assets.
sources: Fed holds rates at 3.50–3.75% in 8-4 vote, most FOMC dissents since 1992 — Federal Reserve / CNN Business

US Q1 2026 GDP expands 2.0% annualized, below 2.3% consensus, up from 0.5% in Q4 2025 — BEA / FXStreet
March 2026 NFP +178K beats estimate of +100K; unemployment steady at 4.3% — BLS Employment Situation
Treasury Q2 2026 Quarterly Refunding due May 4; TBAC documents to reset supply calendar — U.S. Treasury
